text.title: Rotation Evidence Template Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules every rotation-evidence record must enforce so the artefact passes SOC-2 / ISO27001 audit on first inspection.

rule.statement: Every record carries all seven required fields: (1) secret_id (non-sensitive identifier), (2) cause (scheduled | incident | personnel-change | suspected-compromise), (3) initiated_at (ISO-8601 UTC), (4) completed_at, (5) system_of_record_event_id, (6) approver_handles (≥1, dual-control where policy requires), (7) verification_result. A record missing any of the seven is rejected.
rule.rationale: Auditors do not accept "we rotated it on Tuesday". The seven fields together establish what was rotated, why, when, by whose authority, where the system-of-record trail lives, and that downstream consumers were verified. Missing any one field leaves a gap the auditor must close manually.

rule.statement: Once verification_result is recorded, the record is immutable. Corrections require a new record with `supersedes: <prior_record_id>` and a corrected_reason field. Editing a closed record in place is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Mutable evidence is non-evidence. The supersession pattern preserves the original observation while still allowing factual correction; auditors can follow the supersession chain and see the team's response to its own errors.

rule.statement: For secrets classified as high-risk (production database root credentials, code-signing keys, customer-data-encryption KEKs), the record must carry two distinct approver_handles, neither of whom is the operator who performed the rotation. For lower-risk secrets, one named approver is sufficient. The classification is recorded inline so the chosen control level is auditable.
rule.rationale: SOC-2 CC6.1 and ISO27001 A.6.1.2 both require separation of duties for high-risk operations. Inlining the classification means auditors do not need to cross-reference a separate policy doc to confirm the right control was applied.

rule.statement: The record lists each downstream system that consumed the prior secret, each with a verification status: `verified-using-new` | `verified-pinned-version-still-using-old (window-closes-at)` | `decommissioned`. A record with any unverified downstream is rejected as incomplete.
rule.rationale: A rotation is only successful when every consumer has actually switched. The pinned-version-still-using-old state is explicitly named because it is the most common partial-rotation failure mode; forcing a window-closes-at timestamp converts a quiet defect into a tracked obligation.

rule.statement: The record names the prior-secret-decommission timestamp and method (revoked | deleted | grace-window-expired). For grace-window-expired the record must reference the prior r4 reconciliation that confirmed no consumer still used it. A rotation without decommissioning the prior secret is recorded as `decommission: pending` and auto-escalates after the policy window.
rule.rationale: Many "rotations" leave the prior credential valid forever. Auditors specifically test that the prior credential is no longer accepted. Recording the decommission method as a typed enum makes this checkable by tooling, not just by spot-audit.

rule.statement: When cause is `incident` or `suspected-compromise`, the record must carry an `incident_ref` field linking to the incident ticket, AND fields r3/r4/r5 may be filled post-incident within 5 business days. The record opens with `status: provisional` and must transition to `status: closed` once the post-incident fill-in is complete; provisional records older than 5 business days are surfaced on the compliance dashboard.
rule.rationale: During a live compromise, demanding dual-control attestation in real time gets people killed (figuratively — it slows containment). The provisional → closed pattern preserves audit-grade evidence without blocking incident response, and the 5-day window keeps the obligation from being forgotten.

example.description: Minimal closed record for a scheduled production DB password rotation.
example.code: record_id: rot-2026-05-20-pgmain-001 status: closed secret_id: pgmain.app_user classification: high-risk cause: scheduled initiated_at: 2026-05-20T09:00:00Z completed_at: 2026-05-20T09:14:00Z system_of_record_event_id: vault://audit/2026/05/20/op_id=abc123 approver_handles: [@sre-lead, @secops-onduty] operator_handle: @sre-rotation-bot prior_secret_decommission: method: revoked at: 2026-05-20T09:15:00Z downstream_reconciliation: - system: api-prod-1, status: verified-using-new - system: batch-reports, status: verified-using-new - system: legacy-etl, status: verified-pinned-version-still-using-old, window-closes-at: 2026-05-22T00:00:00Z verification_result: pass
